narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger projected outcomes to maintain high bar passage rates. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 1 academic decile ranking of 4th, which stood out as a major concern since students who passed the bar typically ranked around 6th on average—pushing the prediction firmly toward failure. Your LSAT score of 35 also played a significant role in this direction, falling below the average of about 37 for those who succeeded, reinforcing the model's worries about your readiness. On a more positive note, your Year 3 decile of 5th was close to the passers' average of nearly 6th, which helped pull the prediction somewhat toward success and softened the overall risk assessment. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 actually exceeds the average for bar passers at 3.24, and your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who succeeded, these weren't the dominant factors in the model's thinking for you. Being a full-time student is typical for those who pass, matching over 93% of them, so that didn't sway things much either. In comparison to accepted students, your profile shows solid later performance and background alignment but is held back by that earlier decile and LSAT, placing you outside the stronger clusters who typically get approved. This isn't a judgment on your potential or hard work—it's just how these key early indicators stacked up against patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and reflect on strategies to strengthen your foundational academic standing, perhaps through targeted prep courses. You might also explore post-baccalaureate programs or additional undergrad-level coursework to build a more competitive decile profile before reapplying. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact areas, and with your upward trend from Year 1 to Year 3, you're already showing the resilience that could turn this around. Keep pursuing your goals—we're rooting for your next application.
